content.text: LlamaIndex organises all ingested content as

objects. A Document is a container for text content plus a metadata dictionary and a unique ID. Downstream components — indexes, query engines, and retrievers — all operate on Documents or their split-up counterparts,
The top-level unit returned by a loader. Often an entire file, page, or record.
A chunk of a Document created during indexing. Inherits metadata from its parent Document.

# pip install llama-index-core
from llama_index.core import Document

doc = Document(
    text="LlamaIndex is an orchestration framework for LLM applications.",
    metadata={
        "source": "internal_wiki",
        "author": "platform-team",
        "created_at": "2024-01-15",
    },
    # doc_id is auto-generated if omitted (UUID)
)
print(doc.doc_id)      # e.g. "3f4a2b1c-..."
print(doc.metadata)    # {'source': 'internal_wiki', ...}
print(doc.text[:50])   # first 50 chars of content
Documents are the currency of LlamaIndex. Everything you want the LLM to reason about must first become a Document.

is the fastest starting point. It walks a directory, detects file types by extension, and dispatches to the right parser automatically. Supported out of the box: PDF, DOCX, PPTX, CSV, TXT, Markdown, HTML, EPUB, images.

# pip install llama-index-core
from llama_index.core import SimpleDirectoryReader

# Load all files in a directory (recursive)
reader = SimpleDirectoryReader(
    input_dir="./data",
    recursive=True,
    required_exts=[".pdf", ".docx", ".txt"],  # filter by extension
    exclude=["./data/drafts/**"],              # glob patterns to skip
    filename_as_id=True,                       # use filename as doc_id
)
documents = reader.load_data()
print(f"Loaded {len(documents)} documents")
for doc in documents[:3]:
    print(doc.metadata["file_name"], len(doc.text))
attaches file name, file type, file size, creation date, and modification date as metadata. You can add custom metadata per file using a metadata function:

import os

def add_custom_metadata(filename: str) -> dict:
    # Called for every file; return extra metadata to merge
    department = "engineering" if "eng" in filename else "finance"
    return {
        "department": department,
        "env": os.getenv("CONTENT_ENV", "prod"),
    }

reader = SimpleDirectoryReader(
    input_dir="./docs",
    file_metadata=add_custom_metadata,
)
docs = reader.load_data()
# Each doc.metadata now contains both file metadata and custom fields

LlamaHub provides hundreds of community-maintained loaders for external data sources: Notion, Confluence, Jira, Google Drive, GitHub, Slack, web pages, SQL databases, S3, and more. Each loader is a separate pip package.

# pip install llama-index-readers-web beautifulsoup4
from llama_index.readers.web import SimpleWebPageReader

reader = SimpleWebPageReader(html_to_text=True)
docs = reader.load_data(urls=[
    "https://docs.llamaindex.ai/en/stable/",
    "https://docs.llamaindex.ai/en/stable/getting_started/",
])
print(len(docs), "documents")
print(docs[0].text[:200])
, which returns a list of Documents. Implementing your own loader is straightforward:

from llama_index.core.readers.base import BaseReader
from llama_index.core import Document
from typing import List, Optional
import httpx

class JiraIssueReader(BaseReader):
    """Load Jira issues as Documents."""

    def __init__(self, base_url: str, token: str, project: str):
        self.base_url = base_url
        self.headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}
        self.project = project

    def load_data(
        self,
        max_results: int = 100,
        status: Optional[str] = None,
    ) -> List[Document]:
        jql = f"project = {self.project}"
        if status:
            jql += f" AND status = '{status}'"

        resp = httpx.get(
            f"{self.base_url}/rest/api/3/search",
            headers=self.headers,
            params={"jql": jql, "maxResults": max_results},
        )
        resp.raise_for_status()

        docs = []
        for issue in resp.json()["issues"]:
            fields = issue["fields"]
            text = f"{fields['summary']}\n\n{fields.get('description') or ''}"
            docs.append(Document(
                text=text,
                doc_id=issue["key"],
                metadata={
                    "issue_key": issue["key"],
                    "status": fields["status"]["name"],
                    "priority": fields["priority"]["name"],
                    "assignee": (fields.get("assignee") or {}).get("displayName", "unassigned"),
                    "created": fields["created"],
                }
            ))
        return docs

reader = JiraIssueReader(
    base_url="https://yourorg.atlassian.net",
    token="your-api-token",
    project="ENG",
)
docs = reader.load_data(status="In Progress")
For loaders that hit external APIs (web, databases, SaaS tools), loading dozens of documents sequentially is slow. Most LlamaIndex loaders also implement

import asyncio
from llama_index.readers.web import SimpleWebPageReader

async def load_all_pages(urls: list[str]) -> list:
    reader = SimpleWebPageReader(html_to_text=True)
    # aload_data is available on most readers
    docs = await reader.aload_data(urls=urls)
    return docs

urls = [f"https://docs.example.com/page/{i}" for i in range(1, 50)]
docs = asyncio.run(load_all_pages(urls))
print(f"Loaded {len(docs)} docs")
For loaders that do not natively support async, wrap the synchronous call in

import asyncio
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor
from llama_index.readers.database import DatabaseReader

async def parallel_db_load(queries: list[str], uri: str) -> list:
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    reader = DatabaseReader(uri=uri)

    with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=4) as executor:
        tasks = [
            loop.run_in_executor(executor, reader.load_data, q)
            for q in queries
        ]
        results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)

    return [doc for batch in results for doc in batch]
Raw documents often contain content you do not want in the index: navigation menus, footers, cookie banners, or internal notes. Filter at load time — it is cheaper than filtering at retrieval time.
Some metadata fields are useful for post-retrieval filtering (e.g.
) but should not pollute the embedding (their text is not semantically meaningful). Use
to keep the field in the document without including it in the chunk text that gets embedded.

from llama_index.core import Document

doc = Document(
    text="Q3 revenue increased 14% to $2.1B driven by cloud segment growth.",
    metadata={
        "source": "earnings-q3-2024.pdf",
        "department": "finance",
        "page": 12,
        "internal_doc_id": "DOC-8492",  # DB key — irrelevant for search
    },
    excluded_embed_metadata_keys=["internal_doc_id", "department"],
    excluded_llm_metadata_keys=["internal_doc_id"],  # hide from LLM context too
)
Apply text normalization in a post-processing step after loading but before indexing:

import re
from llama_index.core import SimpleDirectoryReader

def clean_text(text: str) -> str:
    # Remove boilerplate headers / footers that repeat on every page
    text = re.sub(r"Page \d+ of \d+", "", text)
    text = re.sub(r"CONFIDENTIAL — INTERNAL USE ONLY", "", text)
    # Collapse excessive whitespace
    text = re.sub(r"\n{3,}", "\n\n", text)
    return text.strip()

reader = SimpleDirectoryReader(input_dir="./reports")
raw_docs = reader.load_data()
cleaned_docs = [
    doc.copy(update={"text": clean_text(doc.text)})
    for doc in raw_docs
    if len(doc.text.strip()) > 100  # drop near-empty pages
]
print(f"{len(raw_docs)} loaded, {len(cleaned_docs)} after cleaning")
Once documents are loaded, the typical next step is to pass them to
which splits, embeds, and stores them. The full load-to-index pattern:

# pip install llama-index-core llama-index-llms-openai llama-index-embeddings-openai
from llama_index.core import SimpleDirectoryReader, VectorStoreIndex, Settings
from llama_index.llms.openai import OpenAI
from llama_index.embeddings.openai import OpenAIEmbedding

Settings.llm = OpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0)
Settings.embed_model = OpenAIEmbedding(model="text-embedding-3-small")

# 1. Load
documents = SimpleDirectoryReader("./docs").load_data()

# 2. Index (splits into nodes, embeds, stores in memory)
index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(documents, show_progress=True)

# 3. Query
query_engine = index.as_query_engine()
response = query_engine.query("What were the key Q3 highlights?")
print(response)
instead of loading documents and building an index in one call. The pipeline adds caching (skip already-processed documents), configurable transformations, and pluggable vector store backends.

# pip install llama-index-core llama-index-vector-stores-qdrant
from llama_index.core import SimpleDirectoryReader
from llama_index.core.ingestion import IngestionPipeline, IngestionCache
from llama_index.core.node_parser import SentenceSplitter
from llama_index.embeddings.openai import OpenAIEmbedding
from llama_index.vector_stores.qdrant import QdrantVectorStore
import qdrant_client

client = qdrant_client.QdrantClient(host="localhost", port=6333)
vector_store = QdrantVectorStore(client=client, collection_name="my_docs")

pipeline = IngestionPipeline(
    transformations=[
        SentenceSplitter(chunk_size=512, chunk_overlap=64),
        OpenAIEmbedding(model="text-embedding-3-small"),
    ],
    vector_store=vector_store,
    cache=IngestionCache(),  # skip docs already in the cache
)

documents = SimpleDirectoryReader("./docs").load_data()
nodes = pipeline.run(documents=documents, show_progress=True)
print(f"Indexed {len(nodes)} nodes")

# Re-run on same docs: cache prevents re-embedding
nodes2 = pipeline.run(documents=documents)
print(f"Re-run: {len(nodes2)} new nodes (should be 0 if no changes)")

hashes each document's content. Unchanged documents produce zero new nodes on re-run. Only new or modified documents are processed — making incremental indexing of a growing document corpus efficient.
for local files and prototyping. For production pipelines or external data sources, pick the matching LlamaHub reader and wrap it in an
with a cache. Write a custom
only when no suitable LlamaHub reader exists.
